elements.sent-2411-003: Evolutionary partial differential equations play a~crucial role in many areas of science and engineering.
elements.sent-2411-004: Spatial discretization of these equations leads to a~system of ordinary differential equations which can then be solved by numerical time integration.
elements.sent-2411-005: Such a~system is often of very high dimension, making the simulation very time consuming.
elements.sent-2411-006: One way to reduce the computational cost is to approximate the large system by a~low-dimensional model using a~model reduction approach and to solve the reduced-order model instead of the original one.
elements.sent-2411-007: This master thesis deals with structure-preserving model reduction of Hamiltonian systems by using machine learning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-008: We discuss a~nonlinear model reduction approach based on the construction of an~encoder-decoder pair that minimizes the approximation error and satisfies symplectic constraints in order to guarantee the preservation of the symplectic structure inherent in Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-009: More specifically, we study an~autoencoder network that learns a~symplectic encoder-decoder pair.
elements.sent-2411-010: Symplecticity poses some additional difficulties, as we need to ensure this structure in each network layer.
elements.sent-2411-011: Since these symplectic constraints are described by the (symplectic) Stiefel manifold, we use manifold optimization techniques to ensure the symplecticity of the encoder and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-012: A~particular challenge is to adapt the ADAM optimizer to the manifold structure.
elements.sent-2411-013: We present a~modified ADAM optimizer that works directly on the Stiefel manifold and compare it to the existing approach based on homogeneous spaces.
elements.sent-2411-014: In addition, we propose several modifications to the network and training setup that significantly improve the performance and accuracy of the autoencoder.
elements.sent-2411-015: Finally, we numerically validate the modified optimizer and different learning configurations on two Hamiltonian systems, the 1D wave equation and the sine-Gordon equation, and demonstrate the improved accuracy and computational efficiency of the presented learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-017: In nature, we find the concept of a~wave in many different places.
elements.sent-2411-018: Just imagine, for example, a~wave travelling in water, the propagation of acoustic waves or a~slackline that is stretched between two trees and set swinging.
elements.sent-2411-019: When we try to describe this physical phenomenon of a~wave mathematically (and make some assumptions for simplification), we get the partial differential equation \begin{equation}\label{waveequationexplained} u_{tt} = \mu^2 u_{\xi \xi}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: Given the slackline example, this can be interpreted as follows: The function $u(t,\xi)$ describes the elevation of the slackline (relative to the resting state) at a~certain position $\xi \in[0,L]$ on the slackline at time $t \in [0,T]$.
elements.sent-2411-021: The factor $\mu > 0$ describes the wave speed.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this context, equation~\eqref{waveequationexplained just means that the acceleration in time equals the acceleration along the position on the slackline multiplied by the squared wave speed.}
elements.sent-2411-023: This correlation originates from the Newtons well-known law $F=ma$, where $F$ denotes the force (that acts in the swinging system), $m$ the mass (of the slackline at a~point) and $a$ the acceleration (in time).
elements.sent-2411-024: What is left to complete the physical mechanism of a~swinging slackline, are the initial and boundary states.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this case, we have the boundary conditions \begin{equation}u(t,0) = u(t,L) = 0,\end{equation} since the ends of the slackline are tied to the tree.
elements.sent-2411-026: Although these boundary conditions can vary for all the examples above, their \enquote{movement is described by equation~\eqref{waveequationexplained .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: This partial differential equation is called 1D linear wave equation.
elements.sent-2411-028: It is an~example of a~so-called Hamiltonian system.
elements.sent-2411-029: Partial differential equations like the 1D linear wave equation can in general not be solved analytically.
elements.sent-2411-030: They are usually reduced to a~ordinary differential equation (ODE) by discretizing the spatial domain.
elements.sent-2411-031: The resulting ODE can then be solved by numerical integration.
elements.sent-2411-032: The problem that we run into here is that the discretization of the spatial domain leads to very high dimensional ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-034: The question is: How can we efficiently solve such a~discretized Hamiltonian system with sufficient accuracy? One way of doing this is a~structure-preserving model reduction.
elements.sent-2411-035: This approach uses so-called encoder and decoder functions to map the Hamiltonian system into a~system of smaller dimension.
elements.sent-2411-036: The crucial point is that in this reduced dimension, we get a~Hamiltonian system again.
elements.sent-2411-037: We then solve the reduced system and recover an~approximate solution of the original system by mapping the reduced solution back to the full dimension using the decoder function.
elements.sent-2411-038: The goal of this thesis is to discuss a~nonlinear approach to determine an~encoder-decoder pair such that we minimize the error of the reconstructed approximate solutions.
elements.sent-2411-039: Precisely, we will use an~autoencoder network to learn the encoder-decoder pair.
elements.sent-2411-040: The conservation of structure requires that the encoder and decoder functions are so-called symplectic functions.
elements.sent-2411-041: This poses some additional challenges, as we need to ensure this structure in each network layer.
elements.sent-2411-042: Since these symplecticity constraints are described by certain manifolds, we will apply manifold optimization techniques to ensure symplecticity of the encoder and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-043: At the beginning, in Section~\ref{manifolds , we give a~brief overview of the most common manifold concepts and the geometric objects needed in later chapters.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We will also focus on how to generalize optimization algorithms to manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section~\ref{sec:modelreduction is the main part of the thesis.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Here, we describe the model reduction of Hamiltonian systems and introduce an~autoencoder network to find the encoder-decoder pair.
elements.sent-2411-047: The network we present was first introduced in \cite{main:brantner2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our goal is to examine the different network layers and discuss some change proposals.
elements.sent-2411-049: Here, we will draw special attention to the manifold update step.
elements.sent-2411-050: More specifically, we will introduce a~new manifold optimization step that generalizes the Adam optimizer to the Stiefel manifold.
elements.sent-2411-051: At the end of this section, we show different learning setups for the network with and without our change proposals.
elements.sent-2411-052: We also explain how we implemented these setups in the Julia programming language.
elements.sent-2411-053: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:numericalexperiments , we present the numerical results for the implemented learning setups for two different examples of Hamiltonian systems.}

elements.sent-2411-060: This section aims to give a~brief summary of the most important concepts of manifold theory.
elements.sent-2411-061: It is intended more as a~reminder than a~complete and clean introduction.

elements.sent-2411-063: To start with, an~$N$-dimensional manifold is a~geometrical object consisting of a~set $\M$ and a~maximal atlas $A^{+}$ of charts from $\M$ to $\R^N$ so that the topology induced by $A^{+}$ on $\M$ is second-countable and Hausdorff.
elements.sent-2411-064: One can think of a~manifold as a~space that locally looks like the Euclidean space $\R^{N}$ in every point.
elements.sent-2411-065: But, whereas the Euclidean space is "straight", the manifold can be curved.
elements.sent-2411-066: There are many ways to discover new manifolds from existing ones.
elements.sent-2411-067: One possibility is to obtain a~manifold $(\Nmann, B^{+ )$, which is a~subset of a~given manifold $(\M, A^{+ )$, as a~so-called embedded submanifold of $\M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: For this, the inclusion map $\iota: \Nmann \to \M$ must be an~immersion and the topology induced by $B^{+}$ needs to equal the subspace topology induced by the ambient space.
elements.sent-2411-069: A~manifold as defined above carries only a~differentiable structure, but we have no information about angles or distances on the manifold.
elements.sent-2411-070: To get this information, we need to add more structure.
elements.sent-2411-071: For this, we endow the tangent spaces of $\M$ at a~point $X \in \M$, denoted by $T_X\M$, with a~smoothly varying inner product \begin{equation}g_X(Z_1,Z_2) := \langle Z_1,Z_2 \rangle_X \quad for all  Z_1,Z_2 \in T_X\M.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: We call the tuple $(\M, g)$ a~Riemannian manifold.
elements.sent-2411-073: We can define a~generalization of the gradient of a~smooth function $f: \M \to \R$.
elements.sent-2411-074: For the Euclidean space $\M=\R^N$, we have $\nabla f(X) := \left(\tfrac{\partial f(X) {\partial X_1 , ...}}
elements.sent-2411-075: We cannot use this, because we have no equivalent for $\tfrac{\partial f(X) {\partial X_i}$ on general manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-076: But we do know that $\nabla f(X)$ is the unique vector satisfying $\langle \nabla f(X), Z \rangle_X = DF(X)[Z]$ for all tangent vectors $Z \in T_X\M$.
elements.sent-2411-077: We use this property to define the Riemannian gradient on a~Riemannian manifold $(\M, g)$ at a~point $X \in \M$ as the unique element $\grad f(X) \in T_X\M$ satisfying \begin{equation}\langle \grad f(X), Z \rangle_X = DF(X)[Z] \quad  for all  Z \in T_X\M.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-078: One problem, we have on (Riemannian) manifolds, is that there is no canonical connection between tangent spaces of different points of $\M$.
elements.sent-2411-079: The so-called affine connection and in particular the unique Riemannian connection (with respect to a~Riemannian metric) establish such a~connection.
elements.sent-2411-080: They generalize the concept of directional derivates to vector fields.
elements.sent-2411-081: Using the Riemannian connection, we can now specify second-order derivates of curves on a~Riemannian manifold.
elements.sent-2411-082: This is realized by acceleration vector fields.
elements.sent-2411-083: We call a~curve on $\M$ with zero acceleration a~geodesic.
elements.sent-2411-084: Geodesics play a~crucial role in many manifold applications.
elements.sent-2411-085: They are uniquely defined by a~starting point and an~initial velocity vector.
elements.sent-2411-086: Using geodesics, we can define the exponential mapping (for a~point $X \in \M$) \begin{equation}\label{sec:manifold_env:align_name:manifoldtheoryexponentialmap} \Exp_X: T_X\M \to \M Z \mapsto \gamma(1,X,Z), \nonumber\end{equation} where $\gamma(1,X,Z)$ denotes the geodesic with starting point $X$ and an~initial velocity vector $Z$ evaluated at time $t=1$.

elements.sent-2411-091: Locally around a~point $X \in \M$, it makes no difference (for the first-order derivative) if we go along a~curve $\beta$ on $\M$ with initial direction $Z$ or if we go in direction $Z$ on the tangent space and then map back to $\M$ via $\Exp_X$: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt} \beta(t)\bigg|_{t=0} = \frac{d}{dt} \Exp_X(tZ)\bigg|_{t=0}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-095: Computation of the parallel transport often requires to solve a~nonlinear ordinary differential equation, which makes it an~expensive task in practice.

elements.sent-2411-142: A~special kind of manifolds are (matrix) Lie groups.
elements.sent-2411-143: These are manifolds with an~additional group structure.
elements.sent-2411-144: In this section, we summarize the main properties of matrix Lie groups and how they interact with other manifolds as a~so-called homogeneous space.
elements.sent-2411-145: The definitions and results used in this chapter are taken from \cite{matrixlie:hall2000 , \cite{top:steimle2021 and \cite{adam:brantner2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Matrix Lie groups are defined as closed subgroups of the group of all invertible real $N\times N$ matrices $\GL(N, \R)$.
elements.sent-2411-147: A~closed subgroup $\G$ of $\GL(N, \R)$ is called a~matrix Lie group.
elements.sent-2411-148: Although this is not at all clear from the definition, (matrix) Lie groups are indeed (smooth) manifolds itself (cf.
elements.sent-2411-149: One of the most important concepts is the Lie algebra of a~matrix Lie group.
elements.sent-2411-151: Then the Lie algebra of $\G$, denoted by $\g$, is the set of all matrices $Z\in \R^{N \times N}$ such that the matrix exponential $e^{tZ}$ is in $\G$ for all real numbers $t$, i.e., \begin{equation}\g = \left\{ Z \in \R^{N \times N} \; \big| \; e^{tZ} \in \G  for all  t \in \R \right\}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-193: This section presents some important matrix Riemannian manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-194: We will have a~close look at some of their main properties and their connection to each other.

elements.sent-2411-196: Second, we introduce the compact Stiefel manifold.

elements.section-2411-003: Model reduction of Hamiltonian systems by a~symplectic autoencoder
elements.sent-2411-305: In physics, we often have to solve partial differential equations (PDEs).
elements.sent-2411-306: A~common approach to solve these systems is to reduce the problem to an~ordinary differential equation (ODE) by using a~spatial discretization.
elements.sent-2411-307: One problem that commonly arises here is that the resulting ODE often is of very high dimension.
elements.sent-2411-308: This makes it numerically expensive to solve.
elements.sent-2411-309: There are various approaches to deal with this issue.
elements.sent-2411-310: In this section, we examine so-called Hamiltonian systems, and we present a~method that allows us to solve these systems efficiently even for high dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-311: First, in Section~\ref{sec:application_subsec:problemsetup , we consider a~solution method for Hamiltonian systems that reduces the system dimension using encoder and decoder functions while preserving the structure of the original system.}
elements.sent-2411-312: In Section~\ref{sec:application_subsec:symplecticautoencodernetwork , we present a~symplectic autoencoder network to determine such a~pair of encoder and decoder functions.}
elements.sent-2411-313: To begin, we describe the exact network setup as it was introduced by \cite{main:brantner2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-314: Then we discuss a~self-created modification of the update step.
elements.sent-2411-315: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:application_subsec:implementation , we show different learning setups of the network that we have implemented in the Julia programming language.}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Structure-preserving model reduction of Hamiltonian systems
elements.sent-2411-316: The goal of this section is to find a~numerically efficient method to solve high dimensional Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-317: A~Hamiltonian system is an~autonomous, i.e. time-independent, ordinary differential equation of a~specific form:.
elements.sent-2411-318: Let $U\subseteq \R^{2N}$ be an~open set and $f:U \to \R^{2N}$ a~Lipschitz-continuous function.
elements.sent-2411-319: The autonomous system $\dot{x = f(x)$ is called Hamiltonian system if there exists a~$C^1$-function $\H:U \to \R$ such that for all $(x_1,x_2) \in U$, the following holds.}

elements.subsection-2411-007: Implementation of different setups to learn the network
elements.sent-2411-499: As part of this thesis, we have implemented different training routines in the Julia programming language to learn the symplectic autoencoder network presented in Section~\ref{sec:application_subsec:symplecticautoencodernetwork .}
elements.sent-2411-500: We have tested the setups for two different examples of Hamiltonian systems.
elements.sent-2411-501: To do so, we used the Julia library GeometricMachineLearning.jl (GML).
elements.sent-2411-502: This library implements the single layers of the network architecture described in Section~\ref{sec:application_subsubsec:architecture .}
elements.sent-2411-503: It contains pre-defined building blocks to chain the layers to a~network, execute an~optimizer step, handle data, conduct the model reduction and much more.
elements.sent-2411-504: The library is developed and maintained by the authors of \cite{main:brantner2023 , who were also the first to introduce this network.}
elements.sent-2411-505: It is still under current development.
elements.sent-2411-506: The latest release version as of June 15th 2024 is v$0.3.0$.
elements.sent-2411-507: For our implementation, we use the release version v$0.2.0$, which we will refer to throughout the rest of the thesis.
elements.sent-2411-508: At some points, we will comment on important changes made in the latest version v$0.3.0$ compared to v$0.2.0$.
elements.sent-2411-509: There also is a~GitHub repository \cite{githubGML:brantner2024 of that project, where one can find the source code, release information and documentations of the project.}
elements.sent-2411-510: We use this library to implement complete learning and testing routines.
elements.sent-2411-511: In this section, we give an~overview of all tested configurations.
elements.sent-2411-512: We omit most of the implementation details here, as they can be found in our accompanying Julia implementation project.
elements.sent-2411-513: We focus on the core parts of the PSDLayer optimizer step and the functional changes made to the GML library modules.
elements.sent-2411-514: First, in Section~\ref{sec:application_subsubsec:generalprojectstructure , we introduce the structure of our implementation project.}
elements.sent-2411-515: Next, we work through the different parts of a~complete learning setup and describe what configurations we implemented.
elements.sent-2411-516: We took the learning configuration used in \cite{main:brantner2023 as a~reference setup.}
elements.sent-2411-517: The authors published their implementation of this setup in \cite[scripts/symplectic\_autoencoder]{githubGML:brantner2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-518: We also show how we have integrated our own network-specific modifications into GML and what adjustments we had to make to GML.

elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{jllisting} # to work with cayley on Stiefel directly: implements vector transport SUBMANIFOLD function cayley!(::SubmanifoldVectortransport, Z::AbstractArray{T}, Φ::AbstractArray{T}, C::AdamCache) where T H = Φ.A'*Z U = hcat(Z - 0.5*Φ.A*(H - H'), -Φ.A) V = vcat(Φ.A',Z') VU = V*U VΦ = V*Φ # apply retraction to update the weights Φ.A .= Φ.A + 0.5*U* ( VΦ + (one(VU) - 0.5*VU) \ (VΦ + 0.5*VU*VΦ) ) # vector transport *sub* of Cache C.B₁ along Cayley H .= Φ.A'*C.B₁ C.B₁ .= C.B₁ - 0.5*Φ.A * ( H + H' ) end # to work with cayley on Stiefel directly: implements vector transport DIFFERENTIAL function cayley!(::DifferentialVectortransport, Z::AbstractArray{T}, Φ::AbstractArray{T}, C::AdamCache) where T H = Φ.A'*Z U = hcat(Z - 0.5*Φ.A*(H - H'), -Φ.A) V = vcat(Φ.A',Z') VU = V*U VΦ = V*Φ # vector transport *diff* of Cache C.B₁ along Cayley H .= Φ.A'*C.B₁ U_B = hcat(C.B₁ - 0.5*Φ.A*(H - H'), -Φ.A) V_B = vcat(Φ.A',C.B₁') VU_B_half = 0.5*(V*U_B) mat_to_be_inv = one(VU) - 0.5*VU C.B₁ .= (U_B + U * (mat_to_be_inv \ VU_B_half)) * (V_B * (Φ.A + U * (mat_to_be_inv \ (0.5*VΦ)))) # apply retraction to update the weights Φ.A .= Φ.A + 0.5*U* ( VΦ + (one(VU) - 0.5*VU) \ (VΦ + 0.5*VU*VΦ) ) end \end{jllisting} \caption{Implementation of the vector transport and the Cayley retraction on $\St(n,N)$.} \label{sec:application_env:jllisting:vectortransports}

elements.sent-2411-601: In the next step we \stexttt{lift the Riemannian gradient to the global tangent space $\g^{hor,E}$, see \eqref{sec:manifold_env:align_name:compactstiefellift+sectionproduct .}}
elements.sent-2411-603: Now, we apply the classic Adam optimizer on an~element of $\g^{hor,E}$.

elements.sent-2411-605: Since the classical Adam optimizer consists only of point-wise operations, we get costs $\O(Nn)$ in the number of non-zero entries of the elements of $\g^{hor,E}$.
elements.sent-2411-606: Finally, we apply the \stexttt{retract operation using the Cayley retraction \eqref{sec:manifold_env:align_name:orthogonalgroupcayleyretraction .}}

elements.sent-2411-609: As before, we first calculate the Riemannian gradient.

elements.sent-2411-612: Second, we apply our proposed \enquote{pseudo-Adam optimizer step described in Algorithm~\ref{sec:application_env:algorithm_name:ModifiedAdam on the compact Stiefel manifold tangent spaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-613: Here, we have to extract the skew-symmetric and the orthogonal components of the tangent vectors and apply point-wise operations.

elements.sent-2411-651: For the ROM setup without the reference state $x_{\reff,\mu}$, this is: \begin{equation}e_{\red}(\mu) = \sqrt{\tfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{K}\norm{x_{\mu}^k-d \left(x_{r, \mu}^k\right)}^2_2 }{ \sum_{k=0}^{K} \norm{x_{\mu}^k}^2_2 }}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-653: The projection error takes the discrete exact solutions of the FOM $(x_\mu^k)_{k=0 ^K$ for a~fixed system parameter $\mu \in \P_{\train}$ and computes the error between the exact solutions and the approximations obtained by the application of the target function $((d \circ e)(x_\mu^k))_{k=0 ^K$.}}
elements.sent-2411-654: For the reference configuration without the reference state $x_{\reff,\mu}$, this is \begin{equation}e_{\proj}(\mu) = \sqrt{ \tfrac{ \sum_{k=0}^{K} \norm{x_{\mu}^k - (d \circ e)\left(x_{\mu}^k\right)}^2_2 }{ \sum_{k=0}^{K} \norm{x_{\mu}^k}^2_2}}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-681: This becomes an~expensive task especially for large iteration numbers $t$.
elements.sent-2411-682: We did not change this for the built-in GML optimizer step since we did not consider the efficiency gain to have a large enough impact to justify the modification of the GML library here.
elements.sent-2411-683: But for our StiefelAdam optimizer step, where we had to implement the optimizer step anyway, we changed this by storing two more Adam parameters $(\beta\_t)_i$ for $i=1,2$, and updating these parameters in every step by a~single multiplication operation \begin{equation}(\beta\_t)_i \leftarrow (\beta\_t)_i \cdot \beta_i\end{equation} implemented in \stexttt{fn\_optimizer.jl .}
elements.sent-2411-684: Note that in GML (and our adjustments) the Adam parameters $\beta_i$ are named $\rho_i$.
elements.sent-2411-685: This becomes very expensive for high dimensions $N \in \N$, especially since the computation of the vector field is needed for the reduced integration.
elements.sent-2411-686: We changed this calculation by using the shape of the Poisson matrix, see Figure~\ref{sec:application_env:jllisting:largematrixmulit .}

elements.sent-2411-688: In this section, we present the numerical results obtained for the different learning setups we tested.
elements.sent-2411-689: First, we compare the two different manifold update step approaches in terms of numerical efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-690: Second, we present the results for the 1D linear wave equation for different learning configurations.
elements.sent-2411-691: Then we do the same for the 1D sine-Gordon equation.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Numerical efficiency of the manifold update step
elements.sent-2411-692: We compared the numerical efficiency of a~single manifold update step using the HomogeneousAdam optimizer implemented in GML to our update step approach using the StiefelAdamOptimizerWithDecay in all implemented Riemannian metric and vector transport combinations.
elements.sent-2411-693: To do this, we created two scripts.
elements.sent-2411-694: The setup of these files is pretty similar: First, we chose a~pair $(N,n)$ of a~very large full dimension $N$ and a~small reduced dimension $n\ll N$.
elements.sent-2411-695: Next, we instantiate the required objects for each optimizer step with standard parameters and initialize a~PSDLayer with the default initial values.
elements.sent-2411-696: Then we add a~one-matrix as the update vector used by the (modified) Adam algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-697: Finally, we conduct two update iterations using the \stexttt{optimization\_step! function defined separately for each setup.}
elements.sent-2411-698: When the function is called for the first time, all objects must be loaded initially.
elements.sent-2411-699: This distorts the actual calculation time of an~update step.
elements.sent-2411-700: Therefore, we first performed a~pre-iteration before we measured the time of an~\stexttt{optimization\_step! iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-701: We displayed these setups in Figure~\ref{sec:application_env:jllisting:speedtestAdamHom and Figure~\ref{sec:application_env:jllisting:speedtestStiefelAdam .}}
elements.sent-2411-702: The test results for different choices of $N$ and $n$ are displayed in Table~\ref{sec:application_env:table_name:speedtestResults .}
elements.sent-2411-703: The calculations were performed on a~NVIDIA Quadro T2000 GPU for Datatype \stexttt{Float64 .}
elements.sent-2411-704: We can observe that all StiefelAdam update step variants are multiple times faster than the homogeneous Adam update step for all tested dimension pairs $(N,n)$.
elements.sent-2411-705: The time difference gets larger the smaller $n$ gets compared to $N$.
elements.sent-2411-706: This is in line with the calculated costs per update step: For one homogeneous space update step, we have costs of $\O(N^2(N-n)+Nn^2)$, see Table~\ref{sec:application_env:table_name:variantsNetwork , whereas one StiefelAdam update step costs $\O(Nn^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-707: Thus, we get the largest speed difference in our test for $N=10000$ and $n=10$.
elements.sent-2411-708: Another interesting observation is that doubling the full dimension $N$ did not cause any significant speed reduction of the StiefelAdam approach for all variants, whereas for the HomogeneousAdam approach, we exceeded the available GPU memory of $4$ Gigabyte, referred to as GPU Out of Memory (GPU OOM), and thus did not get any results.
elements.sent-2411-709: Comparing the different variants of StiefelAdam, we see that the vector transport is the dominating cost factor.
elements.sent-2411-710: The variants using the submanifold vector transport perform worse than the differential vector transport variants for very small reduced dimensions $n$, but perform better for increasing reduced dimensions.

elements.sent-2411-748: First, we aim to reproduce the results published in \cite{main:brantner2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-750: We use 20 different $\mu$ parameters equally spaced over the interval $\P = [\tfrac{5 {12 , \tfrac{2 {3 ]$ for the training set and chose 200 time steps for the discretization of the time interval.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-751: The learning is done for 100 epochs using a~constant batch size of $32$.
elements.sent-2411-752: The first tested variant is V$1$ which is the exact training configuration used in \cite{main:brantner2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-754: These variants all use the homogeneous Adam optimizer implemented in GML.
elements.sent-2411-755: V$2$ implements a~proper epoch-wise learning routine, V$3$ uses an~epoch-wise routine with normalized data and V$4$ performs epoch-wise and normalized learning using an~alternative loss function.
elements.sent-2411-756: All these variants are learned for $100$ epochs.
elements.sent-2411-757: We computed both the reduction errors (see Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_red_errors_N=128_100epochs_PSD+V1-V4 ) and the projection errors (see Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_proj_N=128_100epochs_PSD+V1-V4 ) for these runs and compared them to the PSD method errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-758: The projection errors are quite similar for all runs except for V$4$, where we get a~slightly higher error for small dimensions $\leq 10$ for all test values $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-759: Furthermore, we can observe a~monotonically decreasing error for all variants V$1$-V$4$ in all $\mu$ values.
elements.sent-2411-760: The reduction errors are more interesting.
elements.sent-2411-761: Here, we see the same pattern for all variants.
elements.sent-2411-762: For small reduced dimensions the PSD error values are constantly smaller than the learned autoencoder errors, whereas for reduced dimensions $\geq 10$ the autoencoder errors are almost constantly smaller than the PSD errors.
elements.sent-2411-763: The only exception is $\mu=0.625$ where all variants V$1$-V$4$ perform rather poorly.
elements.sent-2411-764: Comparing the different variants, we can see that the normalized data setup (with the ROM using a~reference state) works best (i.e. variants V$3$ and V$4$).
elements.sent-2411-765: The chosen loss function and the epoch-wise learning setup do not seem to have much impact on the quality of the results.
elements.sent-2411-766: Next, we tested the StiefelAdam optimizer with the same system parameters as before and compared it to the best homogeneous Adam variant V$3$.
elements.sent-2411-767: Since the use of an~alternative loss did not yield any improvements, we decided to use the StiefelAdam optimizer with the GML loss.
elements.sent-2411-768: We started with variant V$5$ using the canonical metric Riemannian gradient and the submanifold vector transport and no decay.
elements.sent-2411-770: Here, we observe something very interesting.
elements.sent-2411-771: Looking at the average loss function value of each epoch (see Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_epoch_losses_N=128_100epochs_V3_V5_V6 ), we see at first a~massive loss decrease that drops from an~initial value around $1$ to a~value around $0.12$ within only four epochs.}
elements.sent-2411-772: This initial decrease is much faster than what we observe for V$3$, where we have an~average epoch loss around $0.62$ after four epochs.
elements.sent-2411-773: Astonishingly, after the third epoch, the epoch loss of V$5$ suddenly increases again and explodes within a~few epochs.
elements.sent-2411-774: This behavior implies that the StiefelAdam optimizer makes good learning progress in the beginning but then gets worse before it goes completely off course.
elements.sent-2411-775: This suggests the assumption that, as we approach a~(local) minimum of the error function, we need to reduce the step size of an~update vector in order to improve.
elements.sent-2411-776: Otherwise, we will just jump over the minimum and drift away.
elements.sent-2411-777: With this in mind, the logical next step is to add a~decay to the learning rate $\eta$ in each step.
elements.sent-2411-778: As described in Section~\ref{sec:application_subsubsec:networkconfiguration , we added the decay $\eta \leftarrow \eta \cdot 0.9995$ in each iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-779: The results of this modification were applied in V$6$.
elements.sent-2411-780: As seen in Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_epoch_losses_N=128_100epochs_V3_V5_V6 , this prevents the epoch loss from exploding, and we get a~monotonous loss decrease.}
elements.sent-2411-781: Nevertheless, we have a~strong bend after four epochs and the loss decrease slows down considerably, so that we are approaching the loss of the homogeneous space optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-782: Further research could be helpful here to investigate the reason for this behavior and thus possibly further improve the performance of the StiefelAdam optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-783: In a~next step, we tested all combinations of the Riemannian metrics and vector transports using the StiefelAdam optimizer with decay (variants V$6$-V$9$) for the same system parameters as above with the only difference that we tested only the reduced dimensions $n=6,7,...,15$.
elements.sent-2411-784: We also tested the canonical metric and submanifold vector transport setup with the alternative loss in V$10$.
elements.sent-2411-785: Since we observed a~very fast loss decrease at the beginning for V$6$, we now learned for only $50$ epochs instead of $100$ epochs.
elements.sent-2411-786: We displayed the reduction error results of these runs in Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_red_errors_N=128_50epochs_PSD+V3_V6_V10 and the average epoch losses in Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_epoch_losses_N=128_50epochs_V3_V6-V10 .}}
elements.sent-2411-787: When comparing to the PSD reduction errors, we can observe that the autoencoders outperform the PSD method for all $\mu$-values except for $\mu=0.625$.
elements.sent-2411-788: Here, we see that the StiefelAdam variants can keep up with the PSD results for reduced dimensions $\geq 11$ whereas the error of variant V$3$ explodes for $n=15$.
elements.sent-2411-789: Comparing the different autoencoder variants, we see that the different StiefelAdam configurations (V$6$-V$9$) work better than the HomogeneousAdam variant V$3$ on average.
elements.sent-2411-790: This is in line with the faster loss decrease for these variants (see Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_epoch_losses_N=128_50epochs_V3_V6-V10 ).}
elements.sent-2411-791: The four different configurations of the Riemannian metrics and vector transports are all pretty similar.
elements.sent-2411-792: We can observe a~slightly better performance for V$8$ (Euclidean metric + submanifold vector transport) and V$9$ (Euclidean metric + differential vector transport).
elements.sent-2411-793: At the end, we performed one more training and testing run for V$9$ in the doubled full dimension $N=256$ and the reduced dimensions $n=10,...,20$.
elements.sent-2411-794: We used the same $\mu$ values as before for the training data and the testing.
elements.sent-2411-795: In Figure~\ref{fig:1Dwave_red_errors_N=256_50epochs_V9vsPSD , we compared the obtained reduction errors to the PSD reduction errors.}
elements.sent-2411-796: We make two interesting observations here.
elements.sent-2411-797: First, for the reduced dimensions $n=10,...,16$, the autoencoder errors can keep up with the PSD errors.
elements.sent-2411-799: Here, we see a massive error decrease for the PSD method, whereas the autoencoder errors increase.
elements.sent-2411-800: We display a~3D-reconstruction comparison for $n=17$ in Figure~\ref{reconstruction comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-801: Second, we see that the autoencoder errors get bigger the larger $\mu$ gets, whereas this is not the case for the PSD errors.
elements.sent-2411-802: Especially for large reduced dimensions between $18$ and $20$, we see that the reduction errors of the autoencoder explode in some cases.
elements.sent-2411-803: This is different from what we observed for $N=128$, where V$9$ could keep up and in most cases outperformed the PSD method for all $\mu$ values tested and reduced dimensions $n\geq 10$.
elements.sent-2411-804: Since the use of a~reduction method is usually applied to very high FOM dimensions $N$, it would be interesting to conduct more experiments for high FOM dimensions to see why the PSD method delivers so much better results than V$9$ here.
elements.sent-2411-805: Another interesting observation is the large integration time for the ROM when using the nonlinear decoder function of the autoencoder compared to the PSD method (see Table~\ref{table:integration_time ).}
elements.sent-2411-806: There may be several reasons for this.
elements.sent-2411-807: On the one hand, of course, this may be due to the fact that the learned autoencoder system already occupies many system resources (memory, etc.) and therefore fewer resources remain for integration, which can lead to a~loss of speed.
elements.sent-2411-808: On the other hand, this may be due to the fact that we have to calculate the derivative of $d$ in each integration step of the ROM.
elements.sent-2411-809: For a~linear function (PSD method) this is an~easy task, but for a~nonlinear decoder $d$ (like the autoencoder functions) this can be quite expensive.

elements.sent-2411-892: This master thesis dealt with the structure-preserving model reduction of Hamiltonian systems with the goal of solving them efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-893: We discussed an~autoencoder network, first introduced by \cite{main:brantner2023 , that learns a~symplectic encoder-decoder pair to conduct the model reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-894: In particular, we presented our own variations of the network and training setup.
elements.sent-2411-895: We paid special attention to the manifold update step of the network and introduced a~new StiefelAdam manifold optimizer step that works directly on the Stiefel manifold by modifying the classical Adam optimizer and avoids the use of homogeneous spaces as proposed in \cite{main:brantner2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-896: We compared the two different approaches (as well as other network and training modifications) and were able to identify some advantages of the StiefelAdam optimizer over the HomogeneousAdam approach.
elements.sent-2411-897: The significantly better numerical efficiency, the faster loss decrease in the first epochs and the better performance for larger variable system parameter values (i.e. $\mu=0.625$ and $\nu= 0.97$) stood out in particular.
elements.sent-2411-898: But we could also observe some drawbacks of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-899: The most surprising drawback is the strong slow-down of the loss decrease after only a~few epochs, which we did not observe for the HomogeneousAdam approach.
elements.sent-2411-900: Further improvements can also be made by using a~more elaborate decay of the learning rate by utilizing information of previous iterations.
elements.sent-2411-901: Testing other system parameter constellations may yield some further improvements.
elements.sent-2411-902: Furthermore, it might also be worthwhile to take a~look at the symplectic preprocessing layers and try to improve in terms of numerical efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-903: This offers many opportunities for further investigation and research on this optimizer and the network in general to address these issues and improve the results.. .
